name: Dario Amodei

llm_review.notes: Scott Wiener is a California State Senator focused on housing/transportation policy, not AI governance. The 'AGI in 2-3 years' claim is an extreme outlier unsupported by mainstream AI research and inconsistent with his actual policy record. Missing critical fields: affiliation, specific policy positions, source/date of data, and evidence supporting timeline claim. Appears to be either misclassified, fabricated, or from unreliable source.

Entry is well-formed with relevant metadata for a U.S. AI policy stakeholder. Scott Wiener is a California State Senator with legitimate AI policy involvement. Data fields are complete and reasonable. Minor consideration: 'Moderate' stance could benefit from citation or specificity, but is defensible given his mixed record on tech regulation.

Entry identifies a real AI policy stakeholder (CA State Senator Scott Wiener, known for tech regulation work), but lacks supporting documentation, verification source, and nuance. The 'Restrictive' stance is oversimplified—Wiener's actual positions are more nuanced (e.g., supports regulation but not blanket bans). Missing: affiliation details, specific policy positions, date submitted, and evidence for AGI timeline claim. Needs citation sources and more granular stance breakdown.

Submission appears to be a test entry with generic placeholder name ('Test Debug Org 4-3') lacking substantive information. Missing critical fields: description, location, focus areas, and any policy-relevant details. Not a legitimate organization entry suitable for a policy stakeholder database.

The name 'Test Pending Org' suggests this may be a test submission rather than a legitimate stakeholder entity. Requires substantial additional data to be useful.

name: Annette Zimmermann
title: Assistant Professor of Philosophy & Affiliate Professor of Statistics
location: Princeton, New Jersey, United States, Berlin, Germany, Madison, Wisconsin, United States
notes: Political philosopher of AI. Book author: 'Democratizing AI' (2026). Argues that 'just because AI is at all of our fingertips, that does not mean it is truly in our hands'. Argues that concentration of wealth and power in the AI market creates a 'democratic legitimacy deficit' because it involves a dispersion of risks and burdens that stands in an asymmetrical relation to the concentration of AI-related benefits. Analyzes 'The Shift': a heterogeneous cluster of recent antidemocratic ideological shifts in Big Tech. Investigates how tech oligarchs and public officials shift democratic agendas and possibility horizons via the mere act of deployment, and argues that 'to deploy is to set agendas', often leading to a narrowing of the policy menu. Comparatively analyzes lobbying/regulatory capture, academic capture and media capture in US and EU AI policy contexts. Uses tools in analytic political philosophy to argue that democratic constituencies can and should exercise more control over AI deployment agendas, e.g. via mechanisms like mini publics, citizen assemblies, measures that strengthen tech workers, and broader economic and legal interventions beyond AI (e.g. strong antitrust enforcement). Argues that 'regular citizens' do not need deep technical expertise to make value-based decisions about AI. Criticizes 'AI exceptionalism' in AI safety/doomer circles and in AI accelerationist/hype circles.
notes_html: Political philosopher of AI. Book author: 'Democratizing AI' (2026). Argues that 'just because AI is at all of our fingertips, that does not mean it is truly in our hands'. Argues that 'regular citizens' do not need deep technical expertise to make value-based decisions about AI. Criticizes 'AI exceptionalism' in AI safety/doomer circles and in AI accelerationist/hype circles.

name: Annette Zimmermann
notes:
Earlier work:
- criticizes 'AI democracy-washing' as an analogous phenomenon to 'AI ethics-washing' 
- argues that humans interacting with AI are predictably 'doxastically negligent' and that one must therefore 'proceed with (epistemic) caution' when making decisions supported by AI 
- argues that academic & policy discourse conflates two notions of AI accountability: one related to explanation and one related to being held responsible 
- criticizes the 'learned helplessness' of tech industry actors and policy actors when it comes to making value-based choices about AI's future trajectory 
- argues that some AI tools which rely on pseudo-scientific assumptions and which have specific nefarious secondary uses should not be built in the first place (this is compatible with the view that other kinds of AI can and should be built) 
- opposes AI design/modeling choices that lead to unfaithful abstractions of complex social phenomena, leading to bad outcomes with respect to AI deployment and AI policy while endowing such outcomes with a false sense of certainty and neutrality 
- opposes technosolutionism on the grounds that such approaches are democratically unaccountable and fail to address deeper societal and economic roots of inequality & bias: argues that technological solutions cannot fix problems like algorithmic injustice 
- early critic of checklist approaches to AI ethics
notes_html:
Earlier work:
- criticizes 'AI democracy-washing' as an analogous phenomenon to 'AI ethics-washing'
- argues that humans interacting with AI are predictably 'doxastically negligent' and that one must therefore 'proceed with (epistemic) caution' when making decisions supported by AI
- argues that academic & policy discourse conflates two notions of AI accountability: one related to explanation and one related to being held responsible
- criticizes the 'learned helplessness' of tech industry actors and policy actors when it comes to making value-based choices about AI's future trajectory
- argues that some AI tools which rely on pseudo-scientific assumptions and which have specific nefarious secondary uses should not be built in the first place (this is compatible with the view that other kinds of AI can and should be built)
- opposes AI design/modeling choices that lead to unfaithful abstractions of complex social phenomena, leading to bad outcomes with respect to AI deployment and AI policy while endowing such outcomes with a false sense of certainty and neutrality
- opposes technosolutionism on the grounds that such approaches are democratically unaccountable and fail to address deeper societal and economic roots of inequality & bias: argues that technological solutions cannot fix problems like algorithmic injustice
- early critic of checklist approaches to AI ethics
